title: Per capita electricity generation from fossil fuels
caption:
Per capita electricity generation from fossil fuels, 2025
Electricity generation from coal, oil, and gas, measured in kilowatt-hours per person.

The choropleth map displays the global distribution of per capita electricity generation derived from fossil fuels, utilizing a continuous color scale to represent values ranging from 0 to 10,000 kilowatt-hours per person. The visualization reveals a distinct geographic concentration of high-intensity fossil fuel usage, with the darkest shades of brown appearing across the Middle East, North America, and parts of the former Soviet Union. Kuwait stands out with the highest visible intensity, falling into the darkest band above 10,000 kWh per person, indicating a heavy reliance on fossil fuels for electricity relative to its population size. Similarly, the United States, Canada, Russia, and Australia occupy the upper tiers of the scale, shaded in deep orange tones that correspond to the 2,000 to 5,000 kWh range. In contrast, the map shows significantly lower per capita generation across the African continent and parts of South Asia, where countries like India and Nigeria appear in the lightest beige shades, representing values below 500 kWh per person. Western Europe presents a mixed pattern, with nations like Germany and Poland showing moderate to high usage in the 1,000 to 2,000 kWh band, while Scandinavian countries and France display lighter shading, reflecting lower fossil fuel dependence. South America generally occupies the middle-to-lower range, with Brazil and Argentina showing moderate levels compared to the global extremes. The data highlights a structural divide where industrialized economies and resource-rich nations in the Northern Hemisphere maintain high per capita fossil fuel electricity generation, while developing regions in the Global South remain at the lower end of the spectrum. The highest values cluster in the Middle East and North America, while the lowest values dominate sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia.
